definition: Coin-operated cabinet or venue where players pay per game; often associated with high scores and leaderboards.
Removable ROM cartridge that stores a game; allowed one console to play many titles (e.g. Atari 2600, NES).
Optical disc format used for games in the 1990s and 2000s; offered much more storage than cartridges.
Game made by a small team or solo developer, often without a major publisher; associated with creative and experimental design.
Genre characterized by procedural generation, permadeath, and turn-based or real-time dungeon exploration.
Digital shop where games are sold or distributed (e.g. Steam, itch.io, eShop).
First-person shooter; genre where the player views the world from the protagonist's eyes and uses weapons.
